coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of a self-generating electricity photoelectrocatalytic system with N-doped carbon quantum dots in BTEX degradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yang
Zhou, Lina
Li, Hui
Zhu, Hailong
Zhang, Teng
Yin, Menglun
Xiang, Qiuyue
Hou, Chen
coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic (PC) degradation process of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) has been consistently restricted by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. To solve this problem, in this study, a novel photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) coupled system was constructed by integrating a single-chamber direct glucose fuel cell (SDGFC), which is capable of self-generating electricity, into the conventional PC system. The coupling system can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes by continuously generating a current without an applied power source. In addition, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared using biomass gelatin. This was employed as a PC material for the catalytic layer of the air cathode in the above coupling system, and the efficient degradation of benzene series (BTEX) was made easy by regulating the content of the N element. Notably, the N-CQDs under the optimal regulation ratio showed excellent optical properties and degradation performance, achieving an impressive PEC degradation rate of over 80 % for BTEX within 180 min. High-performance N-CQDs complement the novel PEC coupling system of self-generating electricity and provide robust support for BTEX degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
BTEX degradation
Photoelectrocatalytic coupled system
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Testing and optimization of SDGFC anode electricity generation performance
Characterization of SDGFC air cathode catalyst N-CQDs
Research on PEC pathways and mechanisms

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic structural diagram of SDGFC (I: electrolyte solution; II: anode electrode material; III: catalyst layer; Ⅳ: fluid collector); (b) the SDGFC device physical drawing.
The TEM images of (a) pure CQDs and (c) N-CQDs; (b) the particle size distribution of N-CQDs; (d) and (e) the high-resolution TEM images of N-CQDs.
(a) and (b) the XRD patterns and (c)the FT-IR pattern of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs.
The XPS full spectra of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs: (a) pure CQDs, (b) N0.1-CQDs, (c) N0.3-CQDs, (d) N0.5-CQDs, (e) N0.8-CQDs and (f) N1-CQDs.
The N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs: (a) pure CQDs, (b) N0.1-CQDs, (c) N0.3-CQDs, (d) N0.5-CQDs, (e) N0.8 −CQDs and (f) N1-CQDs.
The Pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs (a) UV–vis DRS, (b) PL spectrum, and (c) schematic energy band diagram.
(a) The Pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs (a) transient photocurrent response, (b) EIS diagram; (c) Mott-Schottky test of N0.3-CQDs.

The degradation of p-xylene by pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs with (a) degradation curves and (b) rate constants; effect of RH (c) and O2 content (e) on the degradation of p-xylene, (d) and (f) corresponding degradation rate constants.

The degradation of BTEX by N0.3-CQDs (a) degradation curves, (b) primary rate constants, and (c) CO2 mineralization rates; degradation of p-xylene by N0.3-CQDs under different catalytic conditions (d) degradation curves, (e) primary rate constants and (f) CO2 mineralization rates.
The ESR spectra of N0.3-CQDs PEC system: (a) DMPO-·O2 – and (b) DMPO-·OH；(c) BQ, TBA, Na2C2O4 and FFA quenched p-xylene degradation experiment.
The N ls high-resolution XPS spectra at degradation (a) before and (b) after, and (c) the XRD patterns before and after degradation of N0.3-CQDs PEC; (d) the stability of N0.3-CQDs.
The pathway of PEC degradation of p-xylene by N0.3-CQDs.
The mechanism of PEC degradation of p-xylene by N0.3-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of a self-generating electricity photoelectrocatalytic system with N-doped carbon quantum dots in BTEX degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Papermaking Technology and Specialty Paper Development, Key Laboratory of Paper Based Functional Materials of China National Light Industry, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education, Xi’an 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology
Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Papermaking Technology and Specialty Paper Development, Key Laboratory of Paper Based
Functional Materials of China National Light Industry
National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Papermaking Technology and Specialty Paper Development, Key Laboratory of Paper Based Functional Materials of China National Light Industry, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Light Chemistry Engineering Education, Xi’an 710021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic (PC) degradation process of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) has been consistently restricted by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. To solve this problem, in this study, a novel photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) coupled system was constructed by integrating a single-chamber direct glucose fuel cell (SDGFC), which is capable of self-generating electricity, into the conventional PC system. The coupling system can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes by continuously generating a current without an applied power source. In addition, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared using biomass gelatin. This was employed as a PC material for the catalytic layer of the air cathode in the above coupling system, and the efficient degradation of benzene series (BTEX) was made easy by regulating the content of the N element. Notably, the N-CQDs under the optimal regulation ratio showed excellent optical properties and degradation performance, achieving an impressive PEC degradation rate of over 80 % for BTEX within 180 min. High-performance N-CQDs complement the novel PEC coupling system of self-generating electricity and provide robust support for BTEX degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the atmosphere are complex and diverse, such as aldehydes, ketones, olefins, and aromatic compounds [1]. Among these, the benzene series (BTEX), which includes benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene, is a common source of pollution due to its widespread use in industrial solvents, paints, adhesives, and petrol [2]. These substances are highly volatile and toxic, and prolonged exposure or inhalation can severely damage the human respiratory, nervous, and immune systems [3,4], while also polluting the environment and disrupting the ecological balance [5]. Various degradation methods have been devised to protect the environment and address health concerns, such as membrane separation, adsorption, and thermal catalysis. However, these technologies have limitations: Membrane separation is both complex and expensive to operate [6], adsorption necessitates frequent adsorbent replacement [7], and thermal catalysis is energy intensive [8]. In contrast, photocatalytic (PC) technology can use solar energy to degrade pollutants through PC action, which has the advantages of high efficiency, low energy consumption, and no secondary pollution [9,10]. Nevertheless, standalone PC technology faces the challenge of rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, which significantly limits its efficiency in pollutant degradation [11]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop efficient and sustainable BTEX degradation technology to alleviate environmental and health pressures.

It was found that introducing electrical energy into the PC system enables a photoelectrocatalytic (PEC) system that couples PC and electrochemical processes. This can promote electron transfer and effectively inhibit photogenerated electrons and holes recombination [12], thereby enhancing degradation efficiency. For example, Nie et al. [13] degraded formaldehyde 1.76 times more efficiently than a single PC with the PEC system. Wang et al. [14] proposed a bio-PEC reactor to achieve 100 % degradation of ethyl acetate at 50 min. This PEC coupling system offers a potential solution to the challenges faced by PC technology, but the way in which electrical energy is introduced must be considered. If an external power source is introduced, it will increase the energy consumption and the cost. In contrast, a single-chamber direct glucose fuel cell (SDGFC) can generate large amounts of electricity directly from the catalytic oxidation of glucose by electrodes [15]. Coupling it with PC technology is expected to significantly enhance degradation performance without increasing costs, thereby further promoting the application of PC technology in environmental governance.

In this PEC system, the rational selection of photocatalysts is crucial, and among numerous photocatalysts, non-metallic photocatalysts have attracted much attention due to their environmentally favourable and low cost [16]. Especially the quasi-spherical semiconducting materials carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with diameters of less than 10 nm [17]. CQDs have good light absorption in the ultraviolet to near-infrared region, which enables them to efficiently utilize solar light, while their narrow bandgap structure and good electrical conductivity allow them to show advantages in photocatalysis [18]. In contrast, conventional TiO2 only absorbs ultraviolet light and has low solar energy utilization [19], while g-C3N4, although a typical non-metallic photocatalyst, has poor electrical conductivity and is prone to agglomeration [20,21]. Therefore, CQDs, which are prepared in a simple manner, become an ideal alternative material for PC. In preparing CQDs, the selection of precursors is especially critical, and biomass gelatin is ideal due to its wide availability, low cost, non-polluting nature, and ease of processing [22]. Moreover, gelatin is rich in active groups, which can endow CQDs with rich surface chemical functions and facilitate surface modification [23,24]. To further enhance the PC performance of CQDs, nitrogen (N) doping is an inexpensive and easy-to-operate method [25]. N doping can not only effectively modulate the energy band structure and expand the light absorption range, but it can also increase the number of active sites on the surface to facilitate reactant adsorption and reaction conduct, thus enhancing the overall PC activity of the material [26]. Nevertheless, little research has been reported on using N doping to enhance the PC performance of CQDs.

Based on the above discussion, an electrocatalysis (EC) device using SDGFC as the electrical energy supply was constructed and coupled with a PC to form a PEC system, enabling the green and low-energy degradation of BTEX. In this system, an anodic electrolyte is an alkaline glucose solution that generates electricity through catalytic oxidation at anode electrodes, and by regulating its ratio, it is possible to improve the anode's performance in producing electricity. The electrons pass through the external circuit to the air cathode catalyst layer, which is uniformly coated with N-CQDs photocatalysts. N-CQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using gelatin and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (N source), and N0.3-CQDs with high pyridinic N content and excellent optical and degradation properties were obtained by modulating the N doping ratio. The photogenerated electrons and holes were effectively separated by the synergistic effect of the xenon lamp simulating sunlight irradiation, along with the system's own electron production. Based on the available literature, this is the first time that SDGFC is coupled with a PC system for the degradation of BTEX, and this work provides strong support for the efficient degradation of gaseous pollutants by the PEC system.

Gelatin (C12H16O11), ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (ED), benzene (C6H6), toluene (C7H8), ethylbenzene (C8H10), and p-xylene (C8H10) were purchased from Aladdin Co., China. Glucose (C6H12O6), ethanol (C2H6O), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), and potassium hydroxide (KOH) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) micropowder and carbon felt (CF) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. All experimental solutions were prepared with deionized water.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS), Photoluminescence (PL), electron spin resonance (ESR), and electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) were performed in this work. Supporting information (SI), Text S1 shows the detailed characterization processes.

The SDGFC anode consists of an electrolyte solution and anode electrode material. The anode electrode material is 20 × 15 × 1.5 mm nickel foam, and the electrolyte is an alkaline glucose solution. First, 1.5 mol/L KOH was configured using water as a solvent, and 1 mol/L glucose was configured using the configured KOH as a solvent to obtain an alkaline glucose solution. Next, the electric production performance of SDGFC was further explored by regulating the ratio of KOH to glucose.

The SDGFC air cathode comprises three components: the upper carbon base layer, the intermediate fluid collector layer, and the lower catalytic layer. The main function of the fluid collector as the electrode skeleton is to collect electrons, so carbon felt with high durability, good conductivity, and high chemical stability is chosen as the fluid collector. The carbon base layer is loaded on the fluid collector side and is in direct contact with the electrolyte solution. It serves to separate the electrolyte solution from the catalytic layer of the air cathode and, therefore, needs to be superhydrophobic.

The catalytic layer loads the other side of the fluid collector, uniformly coating the photocatalyst N-CQDs. Pure CQDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method using biomass gelatin material. N-CQDs were synthesized under identical experimental conditions, utilizing gelatin and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (ED), and Nx-CQDs were synthesized by adjusting the addition ratio. The photocatalyst was mixed with neutral silica sol, and then the mixed material was evenly applied to the catalytic layer with a brush and placed in an oven for drying. The materials’ preparation details are in Text S2 and S3 in the SI.

The SDGFC, shown in Fig. 1 , uses nickel foam as the anode electrode material for the catalytic oxidation of alkaline glucose to produce electricity. The carbon base layer with superhydrophobic properties is in direct contact with the alkaline glucose solution on the air cathode side. In contrast, the catalytic layer loaded with N-CQDs photocatalyst is directly exposed to air, and the area of this photocatalyst is 4 cm × 7 cm. In order to establish a closed loop, the anode and air cathode were connected via an external circuit. Finally, the reaction device was placed in a sealed cylindrical quartz glass with a volume of 11 cm high by 15 cm in diameter, and the PEC reaction was carried out in this glass reactor. A specific quantity of BTEX solution was injected from the inlet and heated at 80 °C for 30 min in a blast drying oven to completely gasify the solution, following the gas-tightness test that was conducted prior to the commencement of the experiment. After the concentration in the reactor reached equilibrium, the PEC reaction was started, and the concentration of BTEX under light conditions was measured by gas chromatograph every 20 min using a xenon lamp as a sunlight source.
BTEX degradation experiments and detection methods are detailed in SI, Text S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing and optimization of SDGFC anode electricity generation performance
ce:section.ce:para: The electricity generation performance of the SDGFC anode was tested and optimized through experiments, and the results showed that the 1 mol/L glucose solution prepared with 1.5 mol/L KOH obtained a maximum open-circuit voltage of 0.68 V. Under these conditions, the nickel foam anode produced a clean electrical output with a maximum power density of 42.9 mW/m2 and a limiting current density of 221.3 mA/m2 through the oxidation of alkaline glucose (see Text S5 and Fig. S1 for detailed experiments). In the SDGFC PEC system, the green electricity made is enough to give the N-CQDs photocatalysts on the catalytic layer of the air cathode enough electrons, thus effectively separating photogenerated electrons from holes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SDGFC air cathode catalyst N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM allows for observing the morphologies of pure CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a, and N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2c. The overall morphological structure of pure CQDs and N-CQDs is comparable, with both being nearly spherical and devoid of agglomeration, indicating that N doping has not altered the morphological structure of CQDs. The particle size distribution of N-CQDs was further investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. The particle size was approximately within the range of 2 nm to 4 nm, with an average size of 2.4 nm, indicating that the N-CQDs prepared by the one-step hydrothermal method are monodisperse. High-resolution TEM was employed to investigate N-CQDs' microstructure further, as illustrated in Fig. 2d and e. The lattice structure of N-CQDs is clear, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. The spacing aligns with graphite's (100) plane spacing [27,28], suggesting that the N-CQDs have a graphite-like structure.

XRD analyzed the crystal-form structures of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, pure CQDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.72°, indicating that CQDs have an amorphous carbon internal structure, which agrees with what has been reported in the literature [29]. Whereas, after doping with ED as a N source, the characteristic peaks of N source (ED) can be observed in Nx-CQDs. With the increase of N doping, the characteristic peaks of ED were gradually prominent, and the corresponding broad diffraction peaks at 2θ = 23.72° gradually decreased (Fig. 3b). But, as shown in Fig. 3a and Fig. S2, the diffraction peaks belonging to the CQDs could still be observed in the pattern, which indicated that the N doping did not change the structure of the CQDs.

Fig. 3c shows the FT-IR maps of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs, which describe the surface functional groups of the materials. The absorption peaks in the range of 3412 cm−1 are attributed to the telescopic vibration peaks of N–H and –NH2, the absorption peak at 1653 cm−1 is due to the C = O telescopic vibration, and the absorption peak at 1330 cm−1 is attributed to the telescopic vibration of –NH. Compared with pure CQDs, Nx-CQDs showed absorption peaks near 1506 cm−1, attributed to the deformation vibration of N–H, and at 1033 cm−1, attributed to the C-N stretching vibration. The appearance of these absorption peaks of N-containing functional groups proved the successful N doping and synthesis of N-CQDs.

The surface compositions of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs were analyzed using XPS spectroscopy. Peaks of C 1s (284 eV), N 1s (399 eV), and O 1s (531 eV) were observed in the full XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 4 . The figure's pie chart illustrates the content of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, observing that the pure CQDs have a N 1s content of 10.48 % (Fig. 4a). Whereas, when ED with N1s content of 26.47 % (Fig. S3) was used as a N source and doped into the CQDs, it was able to observe a gradual increase in the N 1s content with the increase in N doping. When the ratio of gelatin and ED was 1:1, the content of N 1s was the highest at 17.2 % (Fig. 4f). Meanwhile, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s were analyzed, and as shown in Fig. S4, the C1s was convolved into three peaks, C–C, C-N, and C=O. After introducing the N source into the CQDs, it was observed that the C-N content increased, indicating that the C atoms interacted with more N atoms to form the C-N bond. This result corroborates with the full spectrum of XPS and proves that the N-source doping was successful.

Further, the N1s high-resolution XPS spectra were used to go through the different N configurations in the N-doped carbon materials, which in the usual case consist of three N configurations (pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N) [30]. For the Nx-CQDs (Fig. 5 ), the N 1 s spectra were convolved to two typical peaks, pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, and no graphitic N was found to be present, mainly because graphitic N is usually formed under high-temperature conditions [31].

The table in the figure represents the content of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, showing that as the amount of N doping increases, the content of pyrrolic N initially decreases and then increases, while pyridinic N shows the opposite trend. When the ratio of gelatin to ED is 1:0.3, the highest pyridinic N content is 53.29 % (Fig. 5c); when the ratio is 1:1, the highest pyrrolic N content is 68.76 % (Fig. 5f). It has been shown that pyridinic N significantly affects the performance of N-doped PC materials. This is due to the strong electron-donating ability of pyridinic N species, which can effectively provide Lewis base sites. It makes the photocatalysts enriched with pyridinic N species have superior PC performance in redox reactions [32]. Calculate and convert the proportion of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N content in the N 1 s spectrum to the content of N 1s in the total XPS. As shown in Fig. S5, N0.3-CQDs had the highest pyridinic N content, suggesting that N0.3-CQDs may have better PC performance.

In order to further explore the optimal N doping amount, the optical characteristics of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs were analyzed. First, the optical absorption performance of the PC material was tested through ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS), as shown in Fig. 6 a. They are all located in the visible absorption range, and the UV–vis DRS of Nx-CQDs are red-shifted towards the visible direction compared with pure CQDs. This indicates that N doping enhances visible light absorption, which improves photogenerated electron-hole separation, prolongs photogenerated carrier lifetime, and enhances PC activity. The photon energy to be absorbed by the photoelectron to cross the bandgap to reach the excited state is band gap energy (Eg), which is one of the important indexes for evaluating the optical performance and can be calculated according to Tauc formula (Eq. (1)):
(1)
α h v
1 n
= A
h v -
E g
where α is the absorbance index; h is Planck’s constant; v is the frequency; A is the constant; Eg is the band gap energy.

The Eg values of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs (N0.1-CQDs, N0.3-CQDs, N0.5-CQDs, N0.8-CQDs, and N1-CQDs) were calculated to be 2.39, 2.36, 2.24, 2.30, 2.32, and 2.34 eV, respectively. A schematic band gap diagram is also given in Fig. 6c (the Tauc's band gap plots are shown in Fig. S6), and it can be observed that the Eg values of N-doped CQDs (Nx-CQDs) are all lower than those of pure CQDs. This indicates that N doping can effectively reduce the band gap and improve the optical properties of the material. Meanwhile, it was observed that the N0.3-CQDs exhibited the smallest band gap. This suggests that the PC effect may be better when gelatin: ED = 1:0.3, whereas the agglomeration leads to a decrease in the optical properties of the material after the N doping ratio exceeds 1:0.3 [31].

The transfer and recombination process of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in semiconductors can be evaluated by PL spectroscopy. PL emission mainly originates from the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in semiconductors [33]. Lower fluorescence emission intensity implies a lower photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination rate and higher PC performance [34]. As shown in Fig. 6b, pure CQDs were excited by an excitation light source with a wavelength of 475 nm, and there was an intense fluorescence emission center near 490 nm, indicating that pure CQDs had a higher electron-hole pair recombination rate. Compared with the pure CQDs, the fluorescence emission intensity of the Nx-CQDs is significantly reduced, indicating that the N doping of CQDs has an inhibitory effect on the electron-hole pair recombination. Among them, the N0.3-CQDs exhibited the lowest fluorescence emission intensity, which implies that it has more excellent PC performance, which is consistent with the UV–vis DRS results. ED-containing UV–vis DRS and PL plots are shown in Fig. S7.

The transient photocurrent responses of pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs were measured to evaluate further the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers in the SDGFC air cathode catalyst. As shown in Fig. 7 a, N0.3-CQDs exhibit a significantly higher photocurrent response than pure CQDs and other Nx-CQDs, and the photocurrent does not decay significantly with the increase in exposure times. It is shown that charge transfer and separation will be facilitated at an N doping ratio of 1:0.3, improving the efficiency of electron-hole pair separation. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can verify that N0.3-CQDs have a smaller semicircle radius than pure CQDs and other Nx-CQDs materials (Fig. 7b), which proves that N0.3-CQDs have a faster charge transfer capability. In summary, the N0.3-CQDs (gelatin: ED = 1:0.3) photocatalysts with the highest pyridine N content had the most excellent optical properties, exhibiting the best PC activity. Fig. 7c Mott-Schottky tests were performed on the N0.3-CQDs with the best PC activity, and the Mott-Schottky test is an effective method for determining the semiconductors' flat band potential (Vfb). The Mott-Schottky plot with a positive slope indicates that N0.3-CQDs are n-type semiconductors [35]. According to the intercept between the slope of the fitted straight line and the horizontal axis in the figure, it can be seen that the Vfb of N0.3-CQDs is approximately −0.72 V (−0.48 vs. NHE, NHE = SCE + 0.24 V). For n-type semiconductors, the conduction band potential (ECB) is confirmed by the empirical equation ECB ≈ Vfb-0.1 V [36]. Therefore, the ECB of N0.3-CQDs was determined to be −0.58 V (vs. NHE). Finally, according to the formula (EVB = ECB + Eg) combined with Fig. 6c, it is confirmed that the valence band potential (EVB) of N0.3-CQDs is 1.66 V (vs. NHE) [37].

In this study, p-xylene was selected as the target pollutant, and the PC activity of the materials was evaluated through degradation experiments. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the efficiency of PEC degradation of p-xylene by pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs was explored under general environmental conditions. It was observed that after 40 min of dark adsorption experiments, the concentrations of p-xylene all decreased to a certain extent, indicating that the materials all have certain adsorption properties. The PEC degradation efficiencies of pure CQDs, N0.1-CQDs, N0.3-CQDs, N0.5-CQDs, N0.8-CQDs, and N1-CQDs for p-xylene after 180 min of xenon lamp irradiation were 63.5 %, 65.3 %, 82.3 %, 77.4 %, 74.3 %, and 70.5 %, respectively (Fig. 8a). Among them, N0.3-CQDs had the highest degradation efficiency, which could be corroborated with the previous optical characterization, thus further proving that gelatin: ED = 1:0.3 resulted in the best degradation. Meanwhile, the kinetic constant (K) of N0.3-CQDs was 0.5172 h−1 (Fig. 8b), significantly higher than that of the pure CQDs and the other Nx-CQDs.

In addition, the CO2 content in pure CQDs and Nx-CQDs was measured, and the mineralization rate was calculated for the PEC degradation of p-xylene. As shown in Fig. S8, the mineralization rate of N0.3-CQDs under PEC was 57.1 %, much higher than that of pure CQDs and other Nx-CQDs. The higher mineralization rate implies that more p-xylene can be converted to CO2 and H2O, which further verifies the critical role played by the optimal N-doped amount in promoting the degradation of BETX by PEC.

In order to evaluate the effects of external environmental factors on the PEC degradation of BETX, the effects of relative humidity (RH) and oxygen (O2) content on the PEC degradation process were investigated in this study, using N0.3-CQDs as the photocatalysts and p-xylene as the target pollutant. As shown in Fig. 8c, the p-xylene degradation efficiency increased from 82.8 % to 85.3 % when the RH was increased from 35 % to 40 %. With a further increase in RH, the degradation efficiency showed a decreasing trend, which was attributed to the competition between water molecules and the adsorption of p-xylene, thus shielding the active sites of N0.3-CQDs [38]. Fig. 8d shows the K for the degradation of p-xylene by PEC under different RH conditions, with the maximum value of K (0.5958h−1) at an RH of 40 %. Fig. 8e explores the effect of different O2 contents on the PEC degradation process, and the PEC degradation efficiency increased with increasing O2 content. This is because O2 adsorbed on N0.3-CQDs can be oxidized by photogenerated electrons to produce ·O2 -, which generates more reactive substances to degrade p-xylene. When the O2 content was increased to 30 %, the degradation efficiency was 86.4 %, corresponding to a degradation rate constant of 0.6480 h−1 (Fig. 8(f)). The efficiency of PEC degradation of p-xylene was not further improved with the increase of O2 content, which was mainly attributed to the competition adsorption [39]. When too much O2 is introduced into the PEC system, it leads to competition between O2 and p-xylene adsorption, which reduces the degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst for p-xylene.

By exploring the external environmental factors, it was able to observe that both RH and O2 content affected the degradation of p-xylene by PEC but still had a high degradation rate of up to 82.3 % under general environmental conditions (Fig. 8a). This indicates that PEC has strong stability and adaptability for the degradation of p-xylene under general environmental conditions. Therefore, this study chose to carry out PEC degradation experiments under general environmental conditions. This ensures the reasonableness and realism of the experimental conditions and, at the same time, provides a solid scientific basis for the application of the PEC system in actual environmental pollution control.

The PEC degradation performance of the photocatalysts for different BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and p-xylene) was explored at the optimum N doping (N0.3-CQDs), as shown in Fig. 9 a. N0.3-CQDs showed good PC efficiencies for all of them, among which the highest degradation efficiency (91.2 %) was achieved for ethylbenzene, corresponding to a K value of 0.7092 h−1 (Fig. 9b). Meanwhile, the degradation rates of N0.3-CQDs for all other BTEX were higher than 80 %, indicating that N0.3-CQDs have impressive degradation effects on all BTEX. In addition, the PEC degradation of BTEX was understood by the CO2 mineralization rate, as shown in Fig. 9c. The mineralization rates of N0.3-CQDs on BTEX were all higher than 55 % under the PEC system, with ethylbenzene having the highest mineralization rate (67.6 %). This result further confirms the excellent PC performance of N0.3-CQDs for BTEX degradation.

In order to further verify the degradation effect of PEC, a single PC and EC degradation experiment was carried out using p-xylene as the target pollutant and N0.3-CQDs as the photocatalysts, which were compared with PEC. As shown in Fig. 9d, it can be observed that the efficiency of PEC in degrading p-xylene (82.3 %) is significantly higher than that of PC (57.6 %) and EC (45.3 %). Meanwhile, the K value of PEC was calculated to be 0.5172 h−1, significantly higher than that of PC (0.2466 h−1) and EC (0.1872 h−1), as shown in Fig. 9e. Fig. 9f shows the CO2 mineralization rate under different catalytic conditions. It can be observed that the CO2 mineralization rate of PEC is 57.1 %, which is higher than that of single PC (34.5 %) and EC (22.7 %). This result indicates that single PC or EC degradation is far less effective than coupled PEC in degrading BTEX.

Exploring the active free radicals generated during the degradation of N0.3-CQDs PEC will help to gain a deeper understanding of the PEC degradation mechanism. Therefore, ESR testing determined the species of free radicals generated in the PEC reaction. Fig. 10 a and b show that DMPO-·O2 – and DMPO-·OH signals are almost negligible under dark conditions. Under illumination conditions, both ·O2 – and ·OH have prominent characteristic peaks, indicating that ·O2 – radicals and ·OH radicals are produced in the PEC reaction.

Free radical burst experiments can further verify the generation of active substances by N0.3-CQDs during PEC degradation. Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), tert-butanol (TBA), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were generally used as scavengers to remove photogenerated holes (h+), ·OH, 1O2, and ·O2 –. In order to elucidate the contribution of active species to the degradation of BTEX by N0.3-CQDs PEC, free radical capture experiments were conducted in this study using p-xylene as the target pollutant. The results are shown in Fig. 10c, where the degradation efficiency of p-xylene was significantly decreased by adding Na2C2O4, TBA, and BQ, which were 56.2 %, 48.4 %, and 38.8 %, respectively. In addition, FFA has no apparent inhibitory effect on PEC activity, suggesting that 1O2 is not the primary active substance in the degradation process, which is in keeping with the results of the ESR test (Fig. S9). The above results show that h+, ·O2 – and ·OH play an essential role in the degradation of p-xylene by PEC, and their importance order is ·O2 – > ·OH > h+.

It is known from experiments and related studies that pyridinic N provides electrons more readily than pyrrolic N, making it an essential active site [40]. To better elucidate the role of the N site, the N 1s XPS results of N0.3-CQDs before and after PEC degradation were compared. As shown in Fig. 11 a and b, the N 1s can be fitted to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N peaks. After degradation, the content of pyridinic N was relatively reduced, which suggests that pyridinic N is the leading adsorption site. In addition, the morphology of the PC material N0.3-CQDs was also characterized before and after use. As shown in Fig. 11c, the XRD spectra showed that the crystalline structure of the PC material remained unchanged before and after degradation. Furthermore, the stability of the PC materials was tested by use in cyclic order. As shown in Fig. 11d, the degradation rate of the target pollutant (p-xylene) decreased slightly by about 5 % after five replicate experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on PEC pathways and mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the PEC degradation mechanism of the target pollutant (p-xylene), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) was used to investigate the intermediate products produced during the degradation process. As shown in Fig. S10, benzene, toluene, benzoic acid, and p-xylene peaks appear at different retention times. Combining Fig. S11, previous reports [41], and GC–MS test results, two pathways for PEC degradation of p-xylene were proposed (Fig. 12 ). The main free radicals (·O2 – and ·OH) and h+ attack the 1-methyl or 4-methyl group of p-xylene, and a demethylation reaction occurs to form toluene. The methyl group of toluene is attacked and further oxidized to benzyl alcohol, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid. At the same time, the active material can also attack the 1-methyl and 4-methyl groups of p-xylene, causing the methyl group to fall off to form benzene. A ring-opening reaction then occurs to form a series of intermediates, such as hexanal, hexene, and acetic acid, which are eventually mineralized to CO2 and H2O (Fig. 13 and Table S1) [42].

The impact of superoxide radicals (·O2 –), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and holes (h+) generated during the degradation of p-xylene by N0.3-CQDs on the three systems of EC, PC, and PEC different, so the reaction pathways of these three systems are summarized respectively:
(3)
E C
O 2
+
e -
→
· O
2 -
(4)
PC
N 0.3
- C Q D s + h v →
N 0.3
- C Q D s
(
h vb +
+
e cb -
)
(5)
h
vb
+
+ P - x y l e n e →
CO
2
+
H 2
O
(6)
O 2
+
e
cb
-
→
· O
2
-
(7)
EC/PC
· O
2 -
+ P - x y l e n e → C
O 2
+
H 2
O
(8)
PEC
2
H +
+
· O
2 -
+
e -
/
e cb -
+ h v → 2 · O H
(9)
· O H + P - x y l e n e →
CO
2
+
H 2
O

The above is the catalytic mechanism of N0.3-CQDs in EC, PC, and PEC systems. For EC, since SDGFC generates electricity by itself, its electrons reach the air cathode catalyst layer (N0.3-CQDs) through the external circuit, react with O2 on the catalyst surface to form ·O2 –(Eq. (3)), which can directly catalytically oxidize p-xylene (Eq. (7)) [43]. For PC, electrons undergo a jump when the illumination energy received on N0.3-CQDs is greater than the band gap energy of the forbidden bandwidth. The electrons in the valence band (VB) are excited to the conduction band (CB), forming photogenerated electrons (ecb -) and photogenerated holes (hvb +) (Eq. (4)). Since the ECB of N0.3-CQDs has a more negative reduction potential than that of O2/·O2 – (−0.13 eV vs. NHE), ecb - can reduce O2 to produce ·O2 – to participate in the degradation process (Eq. (6) and Eq. (7)). And hvb + has strong oxidative properties and can directly catalyze the oxidation of p-xylene (Eq. (5)). However, the valence band potential of N0.3-CQDs (1.66 V vs. NHE) is smaller than the oxidation potential of water (H2O→·OH, 1.99 V vs. NHE), so the material is not suitable for direct generation of ·OH from H2O [9]. For PEC, in addition to the two catalytic degradation pathways of EC and PC, the electrons in EC or the ecb - generated by PC can react with ·O2 – under illumination to generate ·OH (Eq. (8)), and ·OH can react with p-xylene reacts to produce CO2 and H2O (Eq. (9)). The above catalytic mechanism can be corroborated with ESR and burst reaction, which can further prove that the degradation efficiency of PEC is better than that of PC and EC.

This study established a green, economical, and mineralization-efficient PEC system. Firstly, the system depends on the current continuously generated by the SDGFC, which is coupled with the PC system, effectively inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Secondly, doping and modulating N elements further optimized the PC material's (Nx-CQDs) performance. N0.3-CQDs with high pyridine N content and excellent optical properties were obtained, and efficient PEC degradation of BTEX was achieved (all degradation rates were higher than 80 %). On the one hand, the coupled system significantly improved the degradation efficiency compared with the PC and EC systems alone. On the other hand, the high pyridine N content helps to increase the active sites and improve the adsorption of BTEX by the PC materials. In addition, GC–MS proposed and analyzed the possible degradation pathways and mechanisms of p-xylene. This work provides strong support for the PEC degradation of gaseous pollutants.

Yang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Lina Zhou: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Software. Hui Li: Software, Validation. Hailong Zhu: Resources, Validation. Teng Zhang: Software, Validation. Menglun Yin: Resources, Validation. Qiuyue Xiang: Software, Validation. Chen Hou: Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22408223); the Chunhui Project Foundation of the Education Department of China (No. HZKY20220519, No. 202200146), and the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (2023-YBSF-335).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.130750.
